race.name: Giro Sardegna Gravel
race.tagline: Southeast Sardinia coastal gravel—107km, fast and non-technical, Mediterranean views, May warmth.

vitals.date_specific: 2026: April 30-May 1 (sources conflict on exact race day — ucigravelworldseries.com/en/sardegna/ says Friday May 1; UCI race-eve article says April 30; both CONFIRMED via UCI, event already ran); 2027 not yet officially announced. PATTERN ESTIMATE: ~early May (per historical late-April/early-May scheduling)

terrain.primary: Southeast Sardinia coastal gravel—fast, rolling, non-technical
terrain.surface: Gravel/paved mix on fast, non-technical coastal roads between Muravera and Villasimius, southeast Sardinia. ~1,220-1,291 m elevation gain over 107 km (sources conflict). Technical rating: 2/5 (current edition; the earlier Siniscola-based editions were more mountainous/technical).

biased_opinion.summary: Giro Sardegna Gravel's current UCI World Series edition runs from Muravera-Villasimius in southeast Sardinia—107 km, fast and non-technical, gain conflicting across sources (~1,220-1,291 m). The event originally launched ~2020 based in Siniscola (NE Sardinia) with a more mountainous, technical profile; it has since relocated. 500-700 riders. May spring conditions (18-28°C). Affordable entry (€90-€130 / $100-$145). If you want Sardinian island gravel with Mediterranean views and affordable entry, this is it. If you need local/domestic, skip it. International destination race.
strengths: Fast, non-technical course—approachable for a wide range of riders
Cagliari airport within reach of the current Muravera-Villasimius venue
weaknesses: Venue changed recently—recon evaporates each edition
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Giro Sardegna Gravel is a Sardinian island adventure now based out of Muravera-Villasimius—107 km, fast and non-technical, Mediterranean views. If you want Sardinian gravel with stunning scenery and affordable entry, this is it. If you need local/domestic, skip it. International travel required but Sardinia makes it worth it. Post-race food/wine.

logistics.alternate_airports: Olbia (OLB) is far from the current Muravera-Villasimius venue; use Cagliari
logistics.lodging_note: Muravera or Villasimius, Sardinia. Book early.
logistics.travel_summary: Fly to Cagliari (CAG). Drive to Muravera or Villasimius (~50-60 km). Coastal town lodging—book early. Day-before packet pickup. Race late April/early May. Post-race Sardinian food/wine.
logistics.food_options: Aid stations on course. Post-race party with Sardinian food/wine.

course_description.character: 107 km. ~1,220-1,291 m elevation (Varies, sources conflict). Fast, non-technical coastal gravel between Muravera and Villasimius, southeast Sardinia.

suffering_zones.name: Throughout: Coastal Gravel
suffering_zones.description: Fast, non-technical coastal gravel and rolling terrain between Muravera and Villasimius. Cumulative elevation still adds up over 107 km even without technical sections.
suffering_zones.survival_strategy: Pace steadily. It's a fast course, so drafting and positioning matter more than technical skill.

suffering_zones.name: Throughout: May Warmth
suffering_zones.description: Late April/early May Sardinia = 20-28°C. Warm. Sun exposure. Mediterranean heat.
suffering_zones.survival_strategy: 3+ bottles. Sun protection. Hydrate constantly. Start early. Warmth is manageable.

course_description.signature_challenge: 107 km of fast, non-technical coastal gravel + May warmth. Mediterranean views between Muravera and Villasimius.

history.origin_story: Giro Sardegna Gravel launched ~2020 based in Siniscola (NE Sardinia) with a mountainous, technical profile. The event has since relocated to a Muravera-Villasimius (SE Sardinia) base with a faster, non-technical 107 km course, 500-700 riders.
history.reputation: Italian gravel scene. Sardinia. Island adventure. Mediterranean views. Well-organized.

prestige.explanation: UCI Gravel World Series qualifier with 30 years of Giro Sardegna history behind it—that's real pedigree. The road version drew legends like Eddy Merckx and Rik Van Looy, so the gravel edition inherits serious cycling credibility. Only in its second year as a gravel race, but it's already positioned as a 'legendary' World Series event. Missing that final prestige point because it's still proving itself in the gravel world.

race_quality.explanation: UCI World Series standards mean proper organization and timing. The pre-relocation Siniscola course (through ~2024) ran a sophisticated 50km-circuit design with beach and dune sections and age-graded distances (72-122km); the event has since relocated to Muravera-Villasimius with a faster, non-technical 107km course. Current-course specifics (feed zones, exact circuit design) are not yet independently verified.

experience.explanation: Mediterranean coastal scenery and Sardinian island adventure carry through the venue change. The pre-relocation Siniscola edition (beach start, Capo Comino, Bidderosa Natural Oasis) is well-documented but describes a course the event no longer runs; the current Muravera-Villasimius edition is a faster, non-technical 107km route along the southeast coast.

community.explanation: Part of a UCI World Series with international field mixing with local Sardinian riders. The event relocated from Siniscola to Muravera-Villasimius; the newer venue's own community/atmosphere is still building since the move.

field_depth.explanation: UCI World Series qualification draws international talent hunting points for World Championships. The established Giro Sardegna reputation pulls quality riders, but as a recently-relocated event (Siniscola -> Muravera-Villasimius) it's still building the elite depth of longer-established World Series races. Expect a strong European contingent given the Mediterranean location.

value.explanation: You're getting Mediterranean island racing with World Series qualification for whatever the entry fee costs. The course design and variety—beach, dunes, dirt circuits, coastal roads—delivers more terrain types than most gravel races manage in twice the distance. Part of a 30-year cycling legacy event, so you're buying into established infrastructure and organization. Plus you're racing through protected natural areas that would cost serious money just to visit as a tourist.

expenses.explanation: Registration runs €90-€130. Sardinia means flying to an island, then getting to the current Muravera-Villasimius venue in the southeast (Cagliari is the nearest major airport, not Olbia, which served the earlier Siniscola-based editions). Accommodation in coastal resort areas during late April/early May hits shoulder season pricing. Similar expense level to Belgian Waffle Ride San Diego.

length.explanation: The current 107 km / 66.5-mile distance hits a moderate gravel-racing length—long enough to matter, short enough not to become a sufferfest. The pre-relocation Siniscola course ran an 81-mile/122km elite distance with age-graded options down to 72km; the current Muravera-Villasimius course's own age-graded structure is not yet independently verified.

technicality.explanation: The current UCI-listed edition out of Muravera-Villasimius is described as fast and non-technical. The pre-relocation Siniscola course (beach-sand starts, dune sections) was more technical, but that course is no longer run. Less technical than most current gravel World Series rounds.

elevation.explanation: Roughly 1,220-1,291 m (sources conflict) of climbing over 107 km on the current Muravera-Villasimius course — moderate, not the 2,600m/8,530ft the old Siniscola-era course carried in this file. Coastal rolling terrain rather than sustained mountain climbing.

climate.explanation: Historical weather data shows average highs of 74°F and lows of 59°F, with a 34% chance of rain on any given day. Late April/early May in Sardinia means shoulder season weather—could be perfect Mediterranean conditions or surprise spring storms. Coastal location brings wind exposure, especially during the beach start and dune sections around Capo Comino. The 'mild winter weather' description suggests generally favorable conditions, but island weather is unpredictable. You're gambling on Mediterranean spring, which usually pays off but offers no guarantees. Similar climate challenges to SBT GRVL.

altitude.explanation: Sea-level coastal start on the current Muravera-Villasimius course means minimal altitude impact on performance. This is pure coastal/low-elevation gravel racing. Lower altitude than Gravel Worlds — no thin-air concerns.

logistics.explanation: Small beach towns like Muravera and Villasimius (the current venue) offer charm but limited infrastructure for big events. Getting to Sardinia requires flight connections (Cagliari is closest) and ground transport to the race base. The event relocated from its original Siniscola base, so logistics details are still settling for the new venue.

adventure.explanation: Coastal gravel through Mediterranean island terrain along Sardinia's southeast coast still delivers real adventure-racing appeal, even without the beach-and-dune sections the pre-relocation Siniscola course was known for (Bidderosa Natural Oasis, Capo Comino Lighthouse — features of the former course, not the current route).

final_verdict.one_liner: Mediterranean gravel with UCI points on a fast, non-technical southeast Sardinia course.
final_verdict.should_you_race: If you want UCI World Series points on a fast, non-technical coastal course with a Mediterranean island backdrop, this delivers. The event relocated from its original Siniscola base to Muravera-Villasimius, so course-specific pacing intel (circuit format, feed zones) is still settling for the new venue. Solid choice if you want UCI World Series points without a technical sufferfest.
final_verdict.alternatives: Gravel Worlds qualifiers like Gralloch in Scotland offer similar points with more technical challenge, while SBT GRVL gives you the big-field energy this lacks. If you want Mediterranean vibes with less travel complexity, consider Tuscany Trail or Spanish gravel events that don't require island logistics.

key_takeaways: The gravel component is newer—it was added as the second edition of the gravel race, with the first edition occurring in 2024

primary.name: Maxxis Reaver
primary.why: Technical all-rounder. EXO+ casing shrugs off rocks and roots.

videos.title: Cala Gonone (Sardegna) DH Track Raw
videos.description: Check out the DH track from Cala Gonone in Sardinia. If you like loose gravel and lots of rocks (limestone) you're in the right place 🤙🤙💪💪 Have cleaned and arranged the track with one of the locals here and it's fun, fast and technical. Enjoy and let me know what you think.

videos.title: Quanta fatica si fa con una Gravel su asfalto? | Scienza in bici
videos.description:
Quanta fatica si fa con una Gravel su asfalto? | Scienza in bici
La gravel ha avuto un boom importante negli ultimi anni e ci sono sempre più persone che la utilizzano anche su strada. Ma a livello di dispendio energetico, di watt per intenderci, che differenza c’è tra una bici gravel e una da strada?
In questo video faremo un test per scoprirlo. 

Guarda il test su Komoot: https://www.komoot.com/tour/918035738

key_challenges.name: East Coast Section
key_challenges.description: Much lumpier than expected with constant up down up down profile rather than flat terrain, despite appearing flat on route maps until zooming in

key_challenges.name: Old Mountain Road
key_challenges.description: Lumpy journey with very little traffic on winding mountain roads

terrain_notes.text: East Coast roads are much lumpier than they appear on route profiles - constant up and down rather than flat sections
Old mountain roads have very little traffic and offer stunning scenery

race_day_tips.text: Start early (around 4am) to avoid the heat of midday and get kilometers done in cooler conditions
Plan to ride into the night when it's cooler rather than fighting strong headwinds and heat during midday
Don't rely on route profiles appearing flat - zoom in close to see the actual elevation changes

additional_quotes.text: It was suggested to me by a guy called Massimo and he's a local Sardinian cycling tour guide and he told me to come this way and I'm really glad he did

rider_intel.search_text: The Sardinian coast offers stunning scenery with crystal blue water and beautiful beaches, but riders should expect much lumpier terrain than route profiles suggest. The East Coast section appears flat on maps but features constant up and down elevation changes that can be challenging. Old mountain roads have very little traffic and provide amazing views. Heat and strong headwinds are significant factors during midday hours. Riders recommend starting very early (around 4am) or riding into the night to avoid the heat. Local knowledge is valuable - connecting with Sardinian cycling tour guides can provide route suggestions for the most scenic roads. The hospitality of Sardinia is notable, with tight-knit communities where everyone knows each other.
